120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md`. 
304	
305	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and avoid conversational filler. Maximum ~100 words total. The plan agent does not need prose; it needs facts.
306	
307	```markdown
308	# Blueprint Writer Report: <slug>
309	**Status:** <COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
310	
311	## Changes
312	- Add `def:foo` (`Foo.bar`): captures XYZ.
313	- Revise `thm:bar`: updated `\uses{}`.
314	- Ref `references/hartshorne.md`: cited smooth criterion.
315	
316	## Notes / Strategy
317	- <ONLY if blocking or critical issue found. Keep to 1 sentence. Omit section if none.>
318	```
319	
320	## Return Value
321	
322	Your final assistant message:
323	
324	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
325	- The path to your full report.
326	
327	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

406	  $\mathscr{D}_1\colon
407	  \mathfrak{C}_g^{[2]}\rightarrow\mathfrak{A}_g\times_{\IA_g}
408	  \mathbb{M}_g$ over $\mathbb{M}_g$ and then the projection of
409	  $\mathfrak{A}_g$. This is a morphism of schemes
410	  $\mathfrak{C}_g\times_{\mathbb{M}_g} S\rightarrow
411	  \mathfrak{A}_g$. Its 
412	  image is a constructible subset of $\mathfrak{A}_g$. 
413	  So it is a union of finitely many 
414	  irreducible Zariski locally closed subsets $\{X_i\}_i$ of
415	  $\mathfrak{A}_g$. We have
416	  the following property. %\Pinlinecomment{I slightly changed the
417	 %   argument here.}
418	
419	  Given a point $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$, there is an $i$ such that
420	  the fiber of $\pi|_{X_i}\colon X_i \rightarrow\mathbb{A}_g$ above
421	  $\tau(s)$ is a finite union of irreducible curves, up-to finitely
422	  many points one of these curves is
423	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s$ with $P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$.
424	  
425	  
426	  We  have a closed immersion $\mathfrak{A}_g\rightarrow \IP_\IQbar^n
427	  \times \mathbb{A}_g$.
428	  Moreover, a sufficiently  large positive power of $\cM$ induces a
429	  closed immersion of $\overline{\mathbb{A}_g}\rightarrow\IP_\IQbar^m$
430	   for some $m$.
431	  Thus, we consider $\mathbb{A}_g$ as a 
432	  Zariski locally closed subset
433	  $\IP_\IQbar^m$. We identify each 
434	  $X_i$ with its image in $\IP^n_\IQbar\times
435	  \IP^m_\IQbar$, an irreducible  Zariski locally closed set.  Then
436	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s \subset\IP^n_\IQbar$ arises as an irreducible
437	  component of the intersection of some Zariski
438	  closure $\overline{X_i}$
439	   with $\IP^n_\IQbar\times
440	  \{\tau(s)\}$.
441	
442	  We use the Segre embedding
443	  $\IP_\IQbar^n\times\IP_\IQbar^m\rightarrow \IP_\IQbar^{(n+1)(m+1)-1}$
444	  to embed our situation into projective space. By B\'ezout's Theorem
445	  \cite[Example~8.4.6]{Fulton},
446	  $\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)$ is bounded from above uniformly in $s$.
447	  Translating a curve inside $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ by a point of
448	  $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$ does not
449	  change its degree. So if $P\in \mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$, then
450	  $\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P)=\deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)$.  
451	  This yields (i).
452	
453	  Part (ii) follows as (i) but this time we use
454	  the Arithmetic B\'ezout Theorem, still executing the intersection after
455	  applying the Segre embedding. Indeed, recall that
456	  $\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s$ as an irreducible
457	  of the intersection of some $\overline{X_i}$ with
458	  $\IP_\IQbar^n\times\{\tau(s)\}$. 
459	  The height and degree of $\overline{X_i}$ are
460	  bounded from above independently of $s$; the same holds for the
461	  degree of $\IP_\IQbar^n \times\{\tau(s)\}$. The height of
462	  $\IP_\IQbar^n \times\{\tau(s)\}$ is bounded from above linearly in terms of
463	  $h(\tau(s))$. Finally, we can apply \cite[Th\'eor\`eme
464	  3]{HauteursAlt3}.
465	  Finally, note that  by the Height
466	  Machine the absolute logarithmic Weil height $h( \tau(s))$,
467	  where $\tau(s)$ is understood as an element of
468	  $\IP^m_{\IQbar}(\IQbar)$, is bounded from
469	  above linearly in terms of $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))$. 
470	\end{proof}

472	\subsection{Non-degeneracy of $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$
473	  for large $M$}  
474	  In this subsection all varieties  are defined over the field $\IC$. 
475	We keep the notation of the previous subsection and let
476	$S$ be an irreducible variety with a quasi-finite %\Pcomment{Changed
477	%  ``finite'' to ``quasi-finite''. Is this OK?}
478	   morphism
479	$S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$. %In this subsection, as in \cite{GaoBettiRank} we take $\IC$ as the base field, \textit{i.e.}, all varieties and morphisms are (viewed as) defined over $\IC$. 
480	Note that
481	$\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is Zariski closed
482	in $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S$ 
483	because $\mathscr{D}_M$ is proper. We endow this image with the
484	reduced induced scheme structure.
485	
486	The following non-degeneracy theorem proved by the second-named author
487	is crucial to prove our main result.
488	It confirms that
489	Theorem~\ref{ThmHtInequality} can be applied to
490	$\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ for $M \ge 3g-2$.
491	We obtain a height inequality on a Zariski open dense subset. 
492	%\Pcomment{I added the comment on the reduced induced structure, as
493	%  proposed by referee .4. FZ is proper as we're using the second
494	%  definition.}
495	
496	\begin{theorem}[\!\!{\cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank}}]\label{ThmNonDegForBd}
497	Let $S$ be an irreducible variety with a (not necessarily dominant) quasi-finite morphism $S \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$. Assume $g \ge 2$ and $M
498	\ge 3g-2$. Then $\mathscr{D}_M( \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]} )$, which is a closed 
499	irreducible subvariety of $\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S$, is 
500	non-degenerate in the sense of
501	Definition~\ref{DefinitionNonDegenerate}.
502	\end{theorem}
503	
504	The fibered product in the theorem involves
505	$S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g\xrightarrow{\tau} \mathbb{A}_g$.
506	
507	More precisely, the meaning of the conclusion of the theorem is as follows. For the abelian scheme $\pi \colon \cA = \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]} \times_{\mathbb{A}_g}S \rightarrow S$ and for the irreducible subvariety $X:=\mathscr{D}_M( \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]} )$ of $\cA$, there exists a open non-empty subset $\Delta$ of $\an{S}$, with the Betti map $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} = \pi^{-1}(\Delta) \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$, such that 
508	\begin{equation}\label{EqNonDegXDMC}
509	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\sman{X}})_x = 2\dim X\quad \text{for some }x \in \sman{X} \cap \cA_{\Delta},
510	\end{equation}
511	when $g \ge 2$ and $M \ge 3g-2$.
512	
513	
514	\begin{proof}
515	This theorem, essentially \cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank}, is a consequence of Theorem~1.3 of \textit{loc.cit.} Because of its importance to the current paper, we hereby give more details of the deduction.
516	
517	We start by showing the result for the case where $\mathfrak{C}_S
518	\rightarrow S$ admits a section $\epsilon$. %\Pcomment{I think we show  something stronger here, namely that 6.2 holds under the weake  assumption that $S\rightarrow\mathbb{M}_g$ is quasi-finite (but with  the additional assumption on the section). Is this correct? And, if yes, does Thm 6.2 hold with $S\rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ quasi-finite?, If so, we should state this.}
519	In this case $\epsilon$ induces an Abel--Jacobi embedding $j_{\epsilon} \colon \mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S)$, which is a closed immersion of $S$-schemes. The modular map is the Cartesian diagram
520	\[
521	\xymatrix{
522	\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S) \ar[r]^-{\iota} \ar[d] \pullbackcorner & \mathfrak{A}_g \ar[d] \\
523	S \ar[r] & \mathbb{A}_g
524	}
525	\]
526	with the bottom morphism being the composite of the given $S \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ with the Torelli map $\tau \colon \mathbb{M}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$. The Torelli map $\tau$ is quasi-finite; see \cite[Lemma 1.11]{OortSteenbrink}. Thus the bottom morphism is quasi-finite. Hence $\iota$ is quasi-finite.
527	
528	We wish to apply \cite[Theorem~1.3]{GaoBettiRank} to the subvariety $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)$ of the abelian scheme $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S) \rightarrow S$. We need to verify the hypotheses. First of all $\iota|_{j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)}$ is generically finite because $\iota$ is quasi-finite. Hypothesis (a) is satisfied since $\dim j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S) = \dim S + 1 > \dim S$. For hypotheses (b) and (c), note that for any $s\in S(\IC)$, the fiber $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)_s$ is the Abel--Jacobi embedding of $\mathfrak{C}_s$ in its Jacobian via the point $\epsilon(s)$. Thus hypothesis (b) is satisfied because each curve generates its Jacobian, and hypothesis (c) holds true since $g \ge 2$.
529	
530	Thus we can apply \cite[Theorem~1.3.(ii)]{GaoBettiRank} and obtain that $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is non-degenerate\footnote{Observe that $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}) = \mathscr{D}_M^{\cA}(j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]}))$, with $\mathscr{D}_M^{\cA}$ be as in \cite[Theorem~1.3.(ii)]{GaoBettiRank} with $\cA = \mathfrak{A}_g\times_{\mathbb{A}_g}S \cong \mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S)$. See below \eqref{eq:prefaltingszhang}.} if $M \ge j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S) = \dim S + 1$. But $\dim S \le \dim \mathbb{M}_g = 3g-3$. Hence  $\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]})$ is non-degenerate if $M \ge 3g-2$.
531	
532	%As $M \ge 3g-2 = \dim \mathbb{M}_g +1 \ge \dim S+1$, \cite[Theorem~1.2']{GaoBettiRank} implies that the theorem holds true if $\mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow S$ admits a section.
533	
534	For an arbitrary $S$, the generic fiber of $\mathfrak{C}_S \rightarrow
535	S$ has a rational point over some finite extension of $K(S)$, the
536	function field of $S$. Thus there exists a quasi-finite \'{e}tale
537	dominant (not necessarily surjective) morphism $\rho \colon S'
538	\rightarrow S$, with $S'$ irreducible, such that $\mathfrak{C}_{S'} = \mathfrak{C}_S \times_S
539	S' \rightarrow S'$ admits a section. Thus $X' :=
540	\mathscr{D}_M(\mathfrak{C}_{S'}^{[M+1]})$, as a subvariety of
541	$\cA':=\mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]}\times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S'$,  is
542	non-degenerate by the previous case. So there exists a connected, open non-empty subset $\Delta'$ of $\an{S'}$, with the Betti map $b_{\Delta'} \colon \cA'_{\Delta'} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$, such that for some $x' \in \sman{X'} \cap \cA'_{\Delta'}$ we have
543	\[
544	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta'}|_{\sman{X'}})_{x'} = 2\dim X'.
545	\]
546	We may furthermore shrink $\Delta'$ so that $\rho|_{\Delta'}$ is a diffeomorphism. In particular $\Delta:=\rho(\Delta')$ is open in $\an{S}$.
547	
548	Denote by $\rho'_{\cA} \colon \cA' = \cA\times_S S' \rightarrow \cA$
549	the projection to the first factor. Then
550	$\rho'_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}}$ is a diffeomorphism. Both $\cA
551	\rightarrow S$ and $\cA' \rightarrow S'$ carry
552	level-$\ell$-structures. By construction and uniqueness properties of
553	the Betti map, we may assume that $b_{\Delta} \colon \cA_{\Delta} \rightarrow \mathbb{T}^{2g}$ equals $b_{\Delta'} \circ  (\rho'_{\cA}|_{\cA'_{\Delta'}})^{-1}$. Thus
554	\[
555	\mathrm{rank}_{\IR} (\mathrm{d}b_{\Delta}|_{\sman{X}})_{x} = 2\dim X'
556	\]
557	with $x = \rho_{\cA}(x')$. So \eqref{EqNonDegXDMC} holds true because $\dim X' = \dim X$. Hence we are done.
558	\end{proof}

562	\subsection{Technical lemmas}
563	
564	
565	The following lemma will be useful in the proofs of the desired
566	bounds. Let for the moment $k$ be an algebraically closed field and
567	$M\ge 1,n\ge 1$ integers. If $Z$ is a Zariski closed subset of $(\IP_k^n)^M$
568	we let $\deg Z$ denote the sum of the degrees of all irreducible
569	components of $Z$ with respect to $\cO(1,\ldots,1)$. 
570	
571	%\marginpar{\tiny{Noga Alon. Should be a rather elementary result.}}
572	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaNAlon} 
573	  Let $C \subset \IP_k^n$ be an
574	  irreducible curve defined over $k$ and let $Z \subseteq (\IP_k^n)^M$
575	  be a Zariski closed subset of $(\IP_k^n)^M$ such that $C^M = C \times
576	  \cdots \times C \not\subseteq Z$. Then there exists a number $B$,
577	  depending only on $M, \deg C,$ and $\deg Z$, satisfying the following
578	  property. If $\Sigma \subset C(k)$ has cardinality $\ge B$, then
579	  $\Sigma^M = \Sigma \times \cdots \times \Sigma \not\subseteq Z(k)$.
580	\end{lemma}
581	\begin{proof}%\Pcomment{I reworded part of this  proof, we don't need flatness or that $C$ is smooth.}
582	  Let us prove this lemma by induction on $M$. The case $M = 1$ follows
583	  easily from B\'{e}zout's Theorem.
584	
585	  Assume the lemma is proved for $1, \ldots, M-1$. Let $q \colon
586	  (\IP_k^n)^M \rightarrow \IP_k^n$ be the projection to the first
587	  factor.
588	
589	  The number of irreducible components of $Z \cap C^M$ and their degrees
590	  are bounded from above in terms of $M,\deg C,$ and $\deg Z$ by
591	  B\'ezout's Theorem applied to the Segre embedding.
592	  Let $Z'$ be the union of all  irreducible
593	  components $Y$ of $Z \cap C^M$ with $\dim q(Y)\ge 1$, let $Z''$ be the
594	  union of all other irreducible components.
595	  % Observe that $q(Y)\subset C$ and $\dim Y \le M-1$ by hypothesis.
596	
597	  Note that $q(Z')\subset C$. For all $P \in C(k)$ the fiber
598	  $q|_{Z'}^{-1}(P)=Z'\cap (\{P\}\times (\IP_k^n)^{M-1})$ has
599	  dimension at most
600	  $\dim Z'-1\le M-2$. So the projection of $q|_{Z'}^{-1}(P)$ to the final
601	  factors $(\IP_k^n)^{M-1}$ does not contain $C^{M-1}$. By B\'ezout's
602	  Theorem the degree of this projection is bounded in terms of $M,\deg
603	  C,$ and $\deg Z$. We apply the induction hypothesis to the projection
604	  of $q|_Y^{-1}(P)$ to $(\IP_k^n)^{M-1}$ and obtain a number $B'$,
605	  depending only on $M,\deg C,$ and $\deg Z$ satisfying the following
606	  property. If $\Sigma \subseteq C(k)$ has cardinality $\ge B'$, then
607	  $\{P\} \times \Sigma^{M-1} \not\subseteq Z'(k)$ for all $P \in C(k)$.
608	  % In particular, $\Sigma^M\not \subset Y(k)$, which concludes this case.
609	
610	  Now $\dim q(Z'') = 0$, so $q(Z'')$ is a finite set of cardinality
611	  at most $B''$, the number of irreducible components of
612	  $Z\cap C^M$. 
613	
614	  The lemma follows with $B=\max\{B',B''+1\}$.  
615	\end{proof}
616	
617	In the next lemma we use the Faltings--Zhang morphism in a single
618	abelian variety $A$, \textit{i.e.}, $\mathscr{D}_M \colon
619	A^{M+1}\rightarrow A^M$ defined by $(P_0,\ldots,P_M)\mapsto
620	(P_1-P_0,\ldots,P_M-P_0)$. 
621	
622	\begin{lemma}\label{LemmaPacketsAlternative3}
623	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety defined over $\overline{\IQ}$ and
624	  suppose $C$ is a smooth curve of genus $g\ge
625	  2$ contained in $A$. If $Z$ is an irreducible Zariski
626	  closed and proper subset of $\mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$, 
627	  then
628	%in $A^M$, then
629	  \begin{equation*}
630	    \#\{ P \in C(\overline{\IQ}) : (C-P)^M \subset Z
631	%    \subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})
632	    \} \le 84(g-1).
633	  \end{equation*}
634	\end{lemma}
635	\begin{proof}
636	For simplicity denote by $\Xi = \{ P \in C(\overline{\IQ}) : (C-P)^M
637	\subset Z \}$. % \subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1}) \}$.
638	%
639	Fix $P_0\in \Xi$. It suffices to prove that there are only $84(g-1)$
640	possibilities for $P_1 - P_0$ when $P_1$ runs over $\Xi$.
641	
642	Say $P_1 \in \Xi$ and let $i\in \{0,1\}$. 
643	Note that
644	$Z\subsetneq \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$ and so
645	 $\dim Z < \dim \mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})\le M+1$, 
646	as $\mathscr{D}_M(C^{M+1})$ irreducible. 
647	Note that $(C-P_i)^M\subset Z$, and so both have dimension $M$.
648	As $Z$ is irreducible we find 
649	 $(C - P_i)^M=Z$ for $i=0$ and $i=1$.
650	
651	Applying the first projection  $A^M\rightarrow A$ yields $C-P_1=C-P_0$. In other words, $P_1-P_0$
652	stabilizes $C$. By Hurwitz's Theorem \cite{Hurwitz1892}, a smooth curve over $\IQbar$ of
653	genus $g\ge 2$ has at most $84(g-1)$ automorphisms.
654	Hence we are done.
655	\end{proof}

3	The goal of this section is to prove the theorems and the corollary in
4	the introduction. To this end let $g\ge 2$; we retain the notation of
5	$\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily}. In particular,
6	$\pi\colon\mathfrak{A}_g\rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$ is the universal
7	family of principally polarized abelian varieties of dimension $g$
8	with level-$\ell$-structure where $\ell\ge \bestlevel$ and
9	$\tau\colon \mathbb{M}_g\rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$ is the Torelli morphism.
10	
11	\begin{proposition}
12	  \label{prop:premazur}        
13	  The exist constants $c_1\ge 0,c_2\ge 1$ depending on the choices made
14	  above with the following property. Let $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$
15	  with $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s)) \ge c_1$. Suppose $\Gamma$ is
16	  a finite rank subgroup of $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$ with rank
17	  $\rho\ge 0$. If $P_0 \in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$, then
18	  $$
19	    \# (\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)-P_0)\cap \Gamma \le
20	    c_2^{1+\rho}.
21	  $$
22	\end{proposition}
23	
24	
25	The proof combines Vojta's approach to the Mordell Conjecture with the
26	results obtained in $\mathsection$\ref{SectionDistanceCurve}. We
27	will use R\'emond's quantitative
28	version~\cite{Remond:Decompte,remond:vojtasup} of Vojta's method. A
29	similar approach was used in the authors's earlier work~\cite{DGH1p}
30	which also contains a review of Vojta's method in $\mathsection$2. Let
31	us recall the fundamental facts before proving Proposition~\ref{prop:premazur}.
32	
33	Suppose we are given an abelian variety $A$ of dimension $g$ that is
34	defined over $\IQbar$ and is presented with a symmetric and very
35	ample line bundle $L$. We assume also that we have a closed immersion
36	of $A$ into some projective space $\IP_\IQbar^n$ determined by a basis
37	of the global sections of $L$. We assume that $A$ becomes a
38	projectively normal subvariety of $\IP_\IQbar^n$. This is the case if
39	$L$ is an at least fourth power of a symmetric and ample line bundle. 
40	
41	Suppose $C$ is an irreducible  curve in $A$. Then let $\deg
42	C$ denote  the degree of $C$ considered as subvariety of
43	$A\subseteq \IP_\IQbar^n$, \textit{i.e.}, $\deg C = (C.L)$. Moreover, let $h(C)$ 
44	denote the height of $C$. 
45	
46	On the ambient projective space  we have the 
47	 Weil height $h\colon
48	\IP_\IQbar^n(\IQbar)\rightarrow [0,\infty)$. Tate's Limit Argument,
49	compare \eqref{eq:fiberwiseNT},
50	applied to $h$ yields the N\'eron--Tate height $\hat h_L\colon A(\IQbar)\rightarrow
51	[0,\infty)$. It vanishes precisely on the points of finite order.
52	Moreover, it follows  from Tate's construction  that 
53	there exists a constant $c_{\mathrm{NT}}\ge 0$, which depends on $A$, such that
54	\begin{equation}
55	  \label{def:cNT}
56	  |\hat h_{L}(P) - h(P)|\le c_{\mathrm{NT}}
57	\end{equation}
58	for all $P\in A(\IQbar)$.
59	
60	Finally, we need a measure for the heights of homogeneous polynomials that
61	define the addition and substraction on $A$, as required in R\'emond's
62	~\cite{remond:vojtasup}. Consider the $n+1$ global sections of $\cO(1)$
63	corresponding to the projective coordinates of $\mathbb{P}_\IQbar^n$.
64	They restrict to  global sections $\xi_0,\ldots,\xi_n$ of
65	$L$ on $A$. Let $f\colon A\times A\rightarrow A\times A$ denote the
66	morphism induced by $(P,Q)\mapsto (P+Q,P-Q)$, and let $p_1,p_2\colon
67	A\times A\rightarrow A$ be the first and section projection,
68	respectively. For all $i,j\in \{0,\ldots,n\}$ there are $P_{ij} \in
69	\IQbar[\mathbf{X},\mathbf{X}']$ with 
70	\begin{equation}
71	  \label{eq:Pijforh1}
72	  f^*(p_1^*\xi_i\otimes
73	  p_2^*\xi_j)=P_{ij}((p_1^*\xi_0,\ldots,p_1^*\xi_n),(p_2^*\xi_0,\ldots,p_2^*\xi_n))
74	\end{equation}
75	and where $P_{ij}$ is bihomogeneous of bidegree $(2,2)$ in $\mathbf{X}
76	= (X_0,\ldots,X_n)$ and $\mathbf{X}' = (X'_0,\ldots,X'_n)$; see 
77	 \cite[Proposition~5.2]{remond:vojtasup} with $a=b=1$
78	% $\mathsection$5.1 and $\mathsection$5.4~\cite{remond:vojtasup} 
79	for the existence of the $P_{ij}$. Here we require that
80	$\xi_0,\ldots,\xi_n$ constitute a basis of $H^0(A,L)$. Let $h_1$
81	denote the Weil height of the point in projective space whose
82	coordinates are all coefficients of all $P_{ij}$.
83	
84	We point out a minor omission in \cite[$\mathsection$2]{DGH1p}: $h_1$
85	there must also involve both addition and subtraction on $A$, and not just
86	the addition.
87	
88	The lemma below is \cite[Corollary~2.3]{DGH1p} which is itself a
89	standard application of R\'emond's explicit formulation of the Vojta
90	and Mumford inequalities. We thus obtain a bound that is exponential in the
91	rank of the subgroup $\Gamma$ for points of sufficiently large
92	N\'eron--Tate height. 
93	
94	\begin{lemma}
95	  \label{lem:vojtamumford}
96	  Let $C$ be an irreducible curve in $A$.
97	There exists a constant $c=c(n,\deg C)\ge 1$ depending
98	only on $n$ and $\deg C$ with the following property. Suppose $\Gamma$
99	is a subgroup of $A(\IQbar)$ of finite rank $\rho\ge 0$. If $C$ is not
100	the translate of an algebraic subgroup of $A$, then
101	\begin{equation*}
102	\# \left \{P \in C(\IQbar)\cap \Gamma : \hat h_{L}(P) >
103	c\max\{1,h(C),c_{\mathrm{NT}},h_1\} \right\} \le c^\rho.
104	\end{equation*}
105	\end{lemma}
106	
107	\begin{proof}[Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:premazur}]
108	  As in $\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily} %we have finitely many
109	%  Zariski open affine subsets
110	%  $V_1,\ldots,V_t$ that cover $\mathbb{A}_g$. Moreover, 
111	  we have a
112	  closed immersion 
113	  $\mathfrak{A}_g \rightarrow\IP^n_\IQbar\times \mathbb{A}_g$ over $\mathbb{A}_g$.
114	  Let $s\in \mathbb{M}_g(\IQbar)$  with
115	  \begin{equation}
116	    \label{eq:hslbc1}
117	    h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s)) \ge \max\{1,c_1\},
118	  \end{equation}
119	  where $c_1$
120	  comes from Proposition \ref{PropAlgPtFar} applied to $S = \mathbb{M}_g$.
121	
122	 % Fix $i\in \{1,\ldots,t\}$ such that $\tau(s)\in V_i(\IQbar)$. 
123	  We now bound two quantities attached to the abelian variety
124	  $A=\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ taken with its closed immersion into
125	  $\IP_\IQbar^n$. Observe that this closed immersion satisfies the
126	  condition imposed at the beginning of this section with $L=\cL|_A$
127	  where $\cL$ is as in $\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily}.
128	  These quantities may depend on $s$. Below, $c>0$ denotes a constant

134	  \textbf{Bounding $c_{\mathrm{NT}}$.} For this we require the

137	  height on $\IP^n_{\IQbar}(\IQbar)$.

139	  % $h_{\overline{\cA},\overline{\cL}}$ in Theorem \ref{thm:silvermantate}.

141	  A(\IQbar)$ we have $|h(P)-\hat h(P)|\le c
142	  \max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}$;
143	  note that we can bound $h_{\overline S}(\pi(P))$ from above linearly
144	  in terms of $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))$ by the Height Machine.
145	  So we may take 
146	  \begin{equation}
147	    \label{eq:choosecNT}
148	    c_{\mathrm{NT}} = c\max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}.    
149	  \end{equation}

151	  \medskip
152	  \textbf{Bounding $h_1$.}\label{page:boundh1} % Let $\xi_0,\ldots,\xi_n$ denote
153	  % global sections of $\cO(1)$ corresponding to 
154	  % projective coordinates of $\IP^n_{\IQbar}$.
155	  Recall that $f\colon A^2\rightarrow A^2$ sends $(P,Q)$ to
156	  $(P+Q,P-Q)$. We know that $P_{ij}$ as above exist. Here we will
157	  construct such a family with controlled height. To this end we
158	  consider points $P=[\zeta_0:\cdots:\zeta_n],Q=[\eta_0:\cdots:\eta_n]
159	  \in A(\IQbar)$. Then $f(P,Q) = ([\nu^+_0:\cdots
160	  :\nu^+_n],[\nu^-_0:\cdots:\nu^-_n])$. Recall that
161	  $A=\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}$ is presented as a projectively normal
162	  subvariety of $\IP^n_\IQbar$ by the construction in
163	  $\mathsection$\ref{subsec:univfamily}. By \eqref{eq:Pijforh1}  there
164	  is for each $i,j\in \{0,\ldots,n\}$
165	  a bihomogeneous polynomial $P_{ij}$ of bidegree $(2,2)$ that is
166	  independent of $P$ and $Q$, with
167	  \begin{equation}
168	    \label{eq:inhomlinearsystemforh1}
169	    \nu^+_i \nu^-_j = \lambda P_{ij}((\zeta_0,\ldots,\zeta_n),(\eta_0,\ldots,\eta_n))
170	  \end{equation}
171	  for some non-zero $\lambda\in\IQbar$ that may depend on $(P,Q)$. 
172	  We  eliminate $\lambda$ and consider  
173	  \begin{equation}
174	    \label{eq:linearsystemforh1}
175	    \nu^+_i \nu^-_jP_{i'j'}((\zeta_0,\ldots,\zeta_n),(\eta_0,\ldots,\eta_n)) - \nu^+_{i'} \nu^-_{j'}P_{ij}((\zeta_0,\ldots,\zeta_n),(\eta_0,\ldots,\eta_n))=0
176	  \end{equation}
177	  as a system of homogeneous linear equations 
178	  parametrized by $(i,j),(i',j')\in \{0,\ldots,n\}^2$, the unknowns are
179	  the coefficients of the $P_{ij}$. As each $P_{ij}$ is bihomogeneous
180	  of bidegree $(2,2)$, the number of unknowns is
181	  $N=(n+1)^2 {n+2 \choose 2}^2$ which is independent of $s$. 
182	
183	  Each pair of points $(P,Q)\in A(\IQbar)^2$ yields one system of
184	  linear equations.
185	  We know that there is a non-trivial solution $(P_{ij})_{ij}$ that solves for all
186	  $(P,Q)$ simultaneously and such that some $P_{ij}$ does not vanish
187	  identically on $A\times A$. Our goal is to find such a common solution of
188	  controlled height.
189	
190	  First, observe that a common solution  for when
191	  $(P,Q)$ runs over all torsion points of $A^2(\IQbar)$ is a common
192	  solution for all pairs $(P,Q)$. Indeed, this follows as torsion
193	  points of $A^2(\IQbar)$ lie Zariski dense in $A^2$. 
194	  Second, observe that the full system has finite rank $M< N$ so it
195	  suffices to consider only finite many torsion points $(P,Q)$.
196	
197	  Our task is thus to find a  common solution to
198	  (\ref{eq:linearsystemforh1}) for all $(i,j),(i',j')$, where some
199	  $P_{ij}$ does not vanish identically on $A\times A$, and where 
200	  $[\zeta_0:\cdots:\zeta_n]$,$[\eta_0:\cdots:\eta_n]$, and
201	  $[\nu^{\pm}_0:\cdots :\nu^{\pm}_n]$ are certain torsion points on
202	  $A(\IQbar)$. We may assume that some $\zeta_i$ is $1$ and similarly
203	  for  $\eta_i$ and
204	  $\nu^{\pm}_i$. So all coordinates are in $\IQbar$. Moreover, the
205	  height  of each torsion point is at most $c_{\mathrm{NT}}$ by (\ref{def:cNT}).
206	  The resulting system of linear equations is represented by an
207	  $M\times N$ matrix with algebraic coefficients. By elementary
208	  properties of the height, each coefficient in the system has affine
209	  Weil height $c \cdot c_{\mathrm{NT}}$, for $c$ large enough. It is tempting, but
210	  unnecessary, to invoke Siegel's Lemma to find a non-trivial solution.
211	  As $M,N$ are bounded in terms of $n$, 
212	  Cramer's Rule establishes the existence of a basis of 
213	  non-zero solution such that the Weil height of the coefficient
214	  vector is at most $c \cdot c_{\mathrm{NT}}$.
215	  Among this basis there
216	  is one solution where one $P_{ij}$ does not vanish identically on
217	  $A\times A$. By \eqref{eq:choosecNT} we find
218	  \begin{equation}
219	    \label{eq:h1bound}
220	    h_1 \le     c\max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}
221	  \end{equation}
222	  for the projective height of the tuple $(P_{ij})_{ij}$. 
223	  Thus \eqref{eq:linearsystemforh1} holds and so we get
224	  \eqref{eq:inhomlinearsystemforh1} on all of $A^2(\IQbar)$, at least with  $\lambda$ a rational
225	  function on $A\times A$ that is not identically zero. But $\lambda$ cannot
226	  vanish anywhere on $A\times A$, as otherwise the left-hand side of
227	  \eqref{eq:inhomlinearsystemforh1} would vanish at some point of
228	  $A\times A$ for all $(i,j)$. Hence $\lambda$ is a non-zero constant. Replacing
229	  $P_{ij}$ by $\lambda P_{ij}$ does not change the projective height;
230	  we get \eqref{eq:Pijforh1} with the desired bound for $h_1$. 
231	
232	  \medskip
233	  \textbf{Bounding height and degree of a curve.} 
234	  By Lemma \ref{lem:uniformdegreebound} we have
235	  \begin{equation}
236	    \label{eq:degreehgtbound}
237	    \deg(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)\le c\quad\text{and}\quad 
238	    h(\mathfrak{C}_s-P_s)\le
239	    c \max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}
240	  \end{equation}
241	  for some
242	  $P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$.
243	
244	  \bigskip
245	
246	  We now follow the argumentation in~\cite{DGH1p}. Let $\Gamma$ be a
247	  subgroup of $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$ for finite rank $\rho$.
248	  We first prove the proposition in the case $P_0=P_s$. We apply Lemma
249	  \ref{lem:vojtamumford}  to the curve $C =
250	  \mathfrak{C}_s-P_s\subset \mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}=A$ and use the bounds
251	  (\ref{eq:choosecNT}), \eqref{eq:h1bound}, and 
252	  (\ref{eq:degreehgtbound}).
253	  Note that $C$ is a smooth curve of genus
254	  $g\ge 2$. So it cannot be the translate of an algebraic subgroup of
255	  $A$.
256	  It follows that the number of points $P\in
257	  \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ with $P-P_s\in \Gamma$ and $\hat h(P-P_s) >
258	  R^2$ where 
259	  \begin{equation}
260	    \label{def:bigR}
261	    R= (c\max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\})^{1/2}    
262	  \end{equation}
263	  is at most  $c^{\rho}\le c^{1+\rho}$.
264	
265	  The burden of this paper is  to find a bound of the same quality 
266	  for the number of pairwise distinct points $P_1,P_2,P_3,\ldots$  in
267	  $\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ with $\hat h(P_i-P_s) \le R^2$. This is where
268	  Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar} enters. Recall our assumption
269	  (\ref{eq:hslbc1}) on $s$.
270	  Let $c_2'$ be the constant $c_2$ from Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar}; it is independent of $s$. As
271	  $\# \Xi_s\le c_2'$ we may assume $P_i\not\in \Xi_s$ for all $i$. 
272	  So we may assume that each $P_i$ is in the second alternative of
273	  Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar}.
274	
275	  As in \cite{DGH1p} we use the Euclidean norm $|\cdot|$ defined by $\hat h^{1/2}$ on
276	  the $\rho$-dimensional  $\IR$-vector space $\Gamma\otimes\IR$. 
277	  Let $r\in (0,R]$. By an elementary ball packing argument, any subset of
278	  $\Gamma\otimes\IR$ contained in a closed ball of radius $R$ is
279	  covered by at most $(1+2R/r)^{\rho}$ closed balls of radius $r$ centered at
280	  elements of the given subset; see \cite[Lemme~6.1]{Remond:Decompte}.
281	  We apply this geometric argument to $R$ as in (\ref{def:bigR}) and
282	  to  $r$, the positive square-root of 
283	  $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))/c_3 =
284	  \max\{1,h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))\}/c_3$.
285	  The contribution of the height
286	  $h_{\overline{\mathbb{A}_g},\cM}(\tau(s))$ cancels in the quotient
287	  $R/r$. We find $R/r\le c$. So the
288	  number of balls in the covering is at most $c^{1+\rho}$. 
289	
290	  By Proposition~\ref{PropAlgPtFar}(ii) the number of the $P_i$'s that
291	  map to a single closed ball of radius $r$ is
292	  at most $c_4$. Thus after increasing $c$ we find that $\#\{P_i \in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)\cap \Gamma : \hat{h}(P_i-P_s) \le R^2\} \le c_4 c^{1+\rho}$, %the
293	%  number of $P_i$ as above is at most $c_4 c^{1+\rho}$, 
294	as desired. 
295	  This completes the proof of the proposition in the case $P_0=P_s$
296	  for sufficiently large $c_2$.
297	
298	  The case of a general base point follows easily as our estimates
299	  depend only on the rank $\rho$ of $\Gamma$. Indeed,
300	  let $P_0\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)$ be an arbitrary point and let
301	  $\Gamma'$ be the subgroup of $\mathfrak{A}_{g,\tau(s)}(\IQbar)$ generated by
302	  $\Gamma$ and $P_0-P_s$. Its rank is at most $\rho +1$.
303	
304	  Now if $Q\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)-P_0$ lies in $\Gamma$, then
305	  $Q+P_0-P_s\in \mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)-P_s$ lies in $\Gamma'$. The
306	  number of such $Q$ is at most $c_2^{2+\rho}$ by
307	  what we already proved. 
308	  The proposition follows as $c_2^{2+\rho}\le (c_2^2)^{1+\rho}$ and
309	  since we may replace $c_2$ by $c_2^2$. 
310	\end{proof}

493	  measuring the complexity of the group law on \(A\) in projective coordinates.

495	  \(h_{\overline{S}}(\pi(P))\), and \cref{lem:uniform_degree_bound} controls

564	  \uses{thm:nondeg_for_bd,thm:ht_inequality_full,lem:uniform_degree_bound,lem:NAlon,lem:packets_alternative3}

\chapter{Jacobian variety of a curve}
\label{chap:jacobian}

% archon:covers MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves/Jacobian.lean

This chapter axiomatizes the Jacobian abelian variety of a smooth genus-\(g\) curve,
the Abel--Jacobi embedding, the Faltings--Zhang morphism, and their key properties.
Because the Picard-group / theta-divisor construction of \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) as a
\(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is not available in Mathlib (Mathlib's
\texttt{WeierstrassCurve.Jacobian} covers only Jacobian coordinates for genus-1
elliptic curves), every declaration in this chapter is treated as an axiom stub;
the \(\lean{}\) targets name the noncomputable Lean declarations to be filled in
the project.

\section{The Jacobian as a principally polarized abelian variety}

\begin{definition}[Jacobian abelian variety]
  \label{def:jacobian_av}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.jacobianAV}
  Let \(g \ge 2\) and let \(C\) be a smooth projective curve of genus \(g\) defined
  over \(\IQbar\), together with a base point \(P_0 \in C(\IQbar)\).
  The \emph{Jacobian} \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) is a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety
  over \(\IQbar\) equipped with the following universal property: for any abelian
  variety \(A\) over \(\IQbar\) and any morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) with
  \(f(P_0) = 0_A\), there exists a unique group homomorphism
  \(\varphi \colon \mathrm{Jac}(C) \to A\) such that \(\varphi \circ \iota_{P_0} = f\),
  where \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) is the Abel--Jacobi embedding
  of \cref{def:abel_jacobi}.

  Concretely, \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) is isomorphic as a group variety to
  \(\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\), the group of degree-zero line bundles on \(C\) modulo
  linear equivalence. This isomorphism is the canonical one sending
  \(\iota_{P_0}(P) \mapsto [\mathcal{O}_C(P - P_0)]\).

  This object is axiomatized (not constructed from the Picard group in this project):
  the existence and universal property of \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) are taken as axioms
  in the Lean formalization.
  % SOURCE: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  %   references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "By definition we have $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g) =
  %   \mathrm{Pic}^0(\mathfrak{C}_g/\mathbb{M}_g)$."
  \textit{Source: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex).}
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Principal polarization of the Jacobian]
  \label{lem:jacobian_ppav}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.jacobianPpav}
  \uses{def:jacobian_av}
  \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) carries a canonical \emph{principal polarization}: a group
  isomorphism \(\theta \colon \mathrm{Jac}(C) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathrm{Jac}(C)^\vee\)
  arising from the theta divisor \(\Theta \subset \mathrm{Jac}(C)\).
  Here \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)^\vee = \mathrm{Pic}^0(\mathrm{Jac}(C))\) is the dual
  abelian variety. In particular, \((\mathrm{Jac}(C), \theta)\) is a principally
  polarized abelian variety of dimension \(g\).
  % SOURCE: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  %   references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Denote by $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_g)$ the relative Jacobian of
  %   $\mathfrak{C}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$. It is an abelian scheme
  %   coming with a natural principal polarization and equipped with level-$\ell$-structure,
  %   see \cite[Proposition 6.9]{MFK:GIT94}."
  \textit{Source: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex).}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jacobian_av}
  This is a standard fact; see \cite[Proposition~6.9]{MFK:GIT94}.
  The theta divisor \(\Theta \subset \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) is defined as the image of
  the \((g-1)\)-th symmetric power \(C^{(g-1)} \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) under the map
  sending an effective divisor \(D\) to \([D - (g-1)P_0]\).
  The line bundle \(\mathcal{O}(\Theta)\) is ample on \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) and
  the induced map \(\mathrm{Jac}(C) \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)^\vee\),
  \(x \mapsto t_x^*\mathcal{O}(\Theta) \otimes \mathcal{O}(\Theta)^{-1}\),
  is an isomorphism (the principal polarization \(\theta\)).
  In the Lean formalization, the existence of \(\theta\) is taken as an axiom.
\end{proof}

\section{The Abel--Jacobi embedding}

\begin{definition}[Abel--Jacobi embedding]
  \label{def:abel_jacobi}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.abelJacobi}
  \uses{def:jacobian_av}
  Fix a base point \(P_0 \in C(\IQbar)\). The \emph{Abel--Jacobi embedding} is the
  morphism
  \[
    \iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \mathrm{Jac}(C), \qquad P \mapsto [\mathcal{O}_C(P - P_0)].
  \]
  In other words, \(\iota_{P_0}(P)\) is the class of the degree-zero divisor \(P - P_0\)
  in \(\mathrm{Pic}^0(C) \cong \mathrm{Jac}(C)\). By construction
  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = 0_{\mathrm{Jac}(C)}\).
  % SOURCE: Section 8 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  %   references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/RatPt.tex)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider the Abel--Jacobi embedding $C-P_0\subset \mathrm{Jac}(C)$
  %   defined over $F$. Then $\# C(F)\le \# (C_{F'}(\IQbar)-P_0)\cap\Gamma = \#
  %   (\mathfrak{C}_s(\IQbar)-P_0)\cap \Gamma$."
  \textit{Source: Section 8 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/RatPt.tex).}
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Abel--Jacobi is a closed immersion]
  \label{thm:abel_jacobi_closed_immersion}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.abelJacobi_closedImmersion}
  \uses{def:abel_jacobi}
  For \(g \ge 2\), the Abel--Jacobi map \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)\)
  is a closed immersion of algebraic varieties.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:abel_jacobi, def:jacobian_av}
  This is a classical result; see, e.g., \cite[Chapter~IV, Theorem~4.2]{ACG:Curve}.
  Injectivity on points follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P) = \iota_{P_0}(Q)\) implies
  \(\mathcal{O}_C(P - Q) \cong \mathcal{O}_C\), i.e., \(P \sim Q\) linearly, which
  for a genus-\(g \ge 2\) curve forces \(P = Q\).
  The differential of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is injective at every point (Torelli-type
  argument), so \(\iota_{P_0}\) is a locally closed immersion; since \(C\) is
  projective and \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) is separated, it is a closed immersion.
  In the Lean formalization, this theorem is taken as an axiom stub.
\end{proof}

\section{The Faltings--Zhang morphism}

\begin{definition}[Faltings--Zhang morphism]
  \label{def:faltings_zhang}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.faltingsZhang}
  \uses{def:jacobian_av, def:abel_jacobi}
  Let \(M \ge 1\) be an integer. The \emph{Faltings--Zhang morphism} is the map
  \[
    \mathscr{D}_M \colon C^{M+1} \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)^M,
    \qquad
    (Q_0, Q_1, \ldots, Q_M) \mapsto (Q_1 - Q_0,\, Q_2 - Q_0,\, \ldots,\, Q_M - Q_0),
  \]
  where the differences \(Q_i - Q_0 := \iota_{Q_0}(Q_i) \in \mathrm{Jac}(C)\)
  are computed via the Abel--Jacobi map based at \(Q_0\) (varying with the tuple).

  In the relative setting of the paper, for a base \(S \to \mathbb{M}_g\) and the
  universal curve \(\mathfrak{C}_S\), this globalizes to a morphism of schemes
  \[
    \mathscr{D}_M \colon \mathfrak{C}_S^{[M+1]} \to
    \mathfrak{A}_g^{[M]} \times_{\mathbb{A}_g} S
  \]
  over \(S\) (the Faltings--Zhang morphism as in \cite[(\ref*{DefFaltingsZhang3})]{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves}).
  This morphism is proper because the diagonal arrow in the defining Cartesian
  diagram is proper.
  % SOURCE: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  %   references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "For $P_0,\ldots,P_M\in C(k)$ the morphism
  %   (\ref{eq:prefaltingszhang}) maps $(P_0,P_1,\ldots,P_M) \mapsto
  %   (P_1-P_0,P_2-P_0,\ldots,P_M-P_0)$ where the difference takes place in
  %   the Jacobian of $C$."
  \textit{Source: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex).}
\end{definition}

\section{Curve generates its Jacobian}

\begin{lemma}[Curve generates its Jacobian]
  \label{lem:curve_generates_jacobian}
  \lean{MR4276287UniformityInMordellLangForCurves.Jacobian.curveGeneratesJacobian}
  \uses{def:abel_jacobi, def:jacobian_av}
  For \(g \ge 2\), the image \(\iota_{P_0}(C) \subset \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) generates
  \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) as an abelian group (or equivalently, as an abelian variety):
  the smallest abelian subvariety of \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) containing \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\)
  is \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) itself.
  % SOURCE: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  %   references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "hypothesis (b) is satisfied because each curve generates its
  %   Jacobian"
  \textit{Source: Section 6 of \cite{MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves} (read from
  references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex).}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:abel_jacobi, def:jacobian_av, lem:jacobian_ppav}
  This is a standard consequence of the definition of the Jacobian.
  Let \(B \subset \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) be an abelian subvariety containing
  \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\). The universal property of \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) applies to
  the inclusion \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to B \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Jac}(C)\)
  and to the composition \(C \xrightarrow{\iota_{P_0}} B\). It gives a unique
  group homomorphism \(\mathrm{Jac}(C) \to B\) splitting the inclusion; combined
  with the inclusion \(B \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Jac}(C)\), this shows
  \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) and \(B\) are isogenous.
  Since \(\dim B \le g = \dim \mathrm{Jac}(C)\) and \(B\) is a subvariety, we
  conclude \(B = \mathrm{Jac}(C)\).
  The property is invoked in the proof of \cref{thm:nondeg_for_bd} (via Gao's
  Theorem~1.3, hypothesis~(b)) to verify that the Abel--Jacobi image satisfies
  the non-degeneracy hypotheses.
  In the Lean formalization, this is axiomatized as an axiom stub.
\end{proof}
